report_metadata.cancer_type: Small cell lung cancer — extensive stage (ES-SCLC)

treatments.name: Atezolizumab + Carboplatin + Etoposide (IMpower133 regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT IMpower133 with 403 patients randomized. Category 1 NCCN recommendation. Replicated in real-world studies. 5-year follow-up data available from IMbrella A extension.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 12.3 vs 10.3 months (HR 0.70, p=0.007). PFS 5.2 vs 4.3 months. 5-year OS rate of 12% vs historical 2% with chemo alone — a 6-fold improvement in long-term survivors. Modest absolute gain but meaningful relative improvement.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved since March 2019 as first-line for ES-SCLC. Universally available at oncology centers worldwide. NCCN Category 1 preferred regimen.

safety_profile.rationale: Standard platinum-doublet toxicity profile: myelosuppression (grade 3-4 neutropenia ~22%), nausea, fatigue, alopecia. Immune-related AEs include rash, hepatitis, hypothyroidism. Manageable with standard supportive care. Diabetes comorbidity requires monitoring for immune-mediated endocrinopathies.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required — benefits all ES-SCLC patients regardless of PD-L1 status. TP53/RB1 mutations are near-universal in SCLC and do not affect eligibility or response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) blocks PD-L1/PD-1 interaction restoring anti-tumor T-cell immunity. Combined with carboplatin (DNA cross-linking) and etoposide (topoisomerase II inhibition) for cytotoxic cell kill. Immunogenic cell death from chemotherapy may enhance checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.

key_evidence.journal: New England Journal of Medicine / Journal of Clinical Oncology

treatments.name: Durvalumab + Platinum-Etoposide (CASPIAN regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT CASPIAN with 805 patients. 3-year OS update confirms sustained benefit. NCCN Category 1 preferred first-line regimen. Allows both cisplatin and carboplatin.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 12.9 vs 10.5 months (HR 0.71, 95% CI 0.60-0.86, p=0.0003). 3-year OS rate 17.6% vs 5.8%. Three times more patients alive at 3 years. Comparable to IMpower133 with slightly better long-term tail.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved since March 2020 for first-line ES-SCLC. Widely available globally. Allows either cisplatin or carboplatin backbone providing flexibility.

safety_profile.rationale: Similar toxicity to atezolizumab-based regimen. Myelosuppression, immune-related AEs. Durvalumab maintenance generally well-tolerated. Addition of tremelimumab did not improve outcomes and increased toxicity — not recommended.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker required. Benefit observed regardless of PD-L1 expression or TMB status. Universal applicability for ES-SCLC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Durvalumab (anti-PD-L1) blocks PD-L1 on tumor cells preventing immune evasion. Combined with platinum-etoposide chemotherapy backbone. Durvalumab maintenance continues after induction cycles up to progression or 24 months.

treatments.name: Tarlatamab (Imdelltra) — DLL3-targeted BiTE

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 DeLLphi-304 demonstrated statistically significant OS benefit over chemotherapy. Phase 2 DeLLphi-301 showed 40% ORR. FDA traditional approval granted November 2025 for 2L ES-SCLC.

survival_benefit.rationale: DeLLphi-304: median OS 13.6 vs 8.3 months (HR 0.60, p<0.001) — a 40% reduction in risk of death. ORR 35% vs 20% with chemotherapy. DOR 6.9 vs 5.5 months. Unprecedented second-line survival for SCLC.

accessibility.rationale: FDA traditional approval November 2025 for adults with ES-SCLC progressed on/after platinum-based chemotherapy. Commercially available as Imdelltra. No companion diagnostic required — DLL3 is expressed in >80% of SCLC.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ AEs 54% vs 80% for chemotherapy — significantly better tolerated. CRS (cytokine release syndrome) is the key AE but manageable with step-up dosing. Less myelosuppression than topotecan or lurbinectedin.

biomarker_match.rationale: DLL3 is expressed in >80% of SCLC tumors. No companion diagnostic required for use. TP53 and RB1 loss are near-universal in SCLC and associated with high DLL3 expression.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tarlatamab is a bispecific T-cell engager (BiTE) antibody construct that simultaneously binds DLL3 on SCLC tumor cells and CD3 on T cells, redirecting cytotoxic T cells to kill DLL3-expressing cancer cells independent of MHC or TCR specificity.

key_evidence.journal: ASCO Annual Meeting / Nature Reviews Clinical Oncology

biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression (present in >80% of SCLC; no companion diagnostic required)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (November 2025) — commercially available as Imdelltra

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin + Atezolizumab Maintenance (IMforte regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 IMforte trial with significant PFS and OS improvements over atezolizumab maintenance alone. sNDA planned for first-line maintenance setting.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 13.2 vs 10.6 months for lurbinectedin+atezolizumab vs atezolizumab alone as maintenance. Median PFS 5.4 vs 2.1 months — a 2.5-fold improvement in PFS. Meaningful extension of first-line benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Not yet FDA approved as a combination for maintenance. sNDA submission planned H1 2025. Both individual agents are commercially available. Phase 3 data presented but awaiting regulatory review.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination adds myelosuppressive toxicity of lurbinectedin to atezolizumab immune-related AEs. Grade 3-4 neutropenia is the primary concern. Requires G-CSF support in many patients.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker selection required. All ES-SCLC patients who respond to first-line chemo-immunotherapy are eligible.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Combines lurbinectedin's transcription inhibition and tumor-associated macrophage depletion with atezolizumab's PD-L1 checkpoint blockade. Lurbinectedin's immunomodulatory effect on the tumor microenvironment may synergize with continued immune checkpoint inhibition during maintenance.
treatments.availability: Phase 3 trial completed — sNDA planned H1 2025, awaiting FDA approval

treatments.name: Lurbinectedin (Zepzelca) — Second-Line Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: FDA accelerated approval based on Phase 2 single-arm study (Study B-005). Phase 3 LAGOON trial ongoing with results expected Q1 2026. Real-world evidence supports activity in second-line setting.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 35% in second-line (higher than topotecan ~24%). Median OS approximately 9.3 months. Median DOR 5.3 months. Activity seen regardless of platinum sensitivity. Now superseded by tarlatamab in 2L based on DeLLphi-304 data.

accessibility.rationale: FDA accelerated approval June 2020 for ES-SCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy. Commercially available as Zepzelca. Widely accessible in the US and expanding globally.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 neutropenia (~46%), anemia (~9%), thrombocytopenia (~7%). Hepatotoxicity requires monitoring. Fatigue common. Myelosuppression is dose-limiting — may be mitigated with trilaciclib.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker required. Activity independent of TP53/RB1 status. Works across all SCLC subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lurbinectedin is a synthetic alkaloid that inhibits oncogenic transcription by binding to DNA minor groove, blocking RNA polymerase II, and inducing DNA double-strand breaks. It also modulates the tumor microenvironment by depleting tumor-associated macrophages.
treatments.availability: FDA Accelerated Approval (June 2020) — commercially available as Zepzelca

treatments.name: Trilaciclib (Cosela) + Chemotherapy — Myelopreservation

evidence_level.rationale: FDA approved based on 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials in ES-SCLC. Added to NCCN guidelines. Unique first-in-class mechanism with robust evidence for myelopreservation.

survival_benefit.rationale: Primary endpoint is myelopreservation, not direct anti-tumor activity. Reduces grade 4 neutropenia from ~50% to ~2%. May enable chemotherapy dose intensity maintenance. Exploratory OS signals but not powered for survival endpoints.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved February 2021 for ES-SCLC. Commercially available as Cosela. Administered IV before each chemotherapy dose. Included in NCCN guidelines.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated. Designed to reduce toxicity rather than add it. Most common AEs are fatigue, hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, headache. Dramatically reduces chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression.

biomarker_match.rationale: Works by protecting CDK4/6-dependent hematopoietic cells. SCLC cells with RB1 loss are CDK4/6-independent, making them insensitive to trilaciclib — ideal selective protection. This patient's RB1 loss ensures tumor bypass.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trilaciclib is a transient CDK4/6 inhibitor administered IV before chemotherapy to induce reversible G1 arrest in hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells and immune cells, protecting them from chemotherapy damage. RB1-deficient SCLC cells are CDK4/6-independent and remain sensitive to chemotherapy.
biomarker_requirements: RB1 loss preferred (ensures tumor cells are CDK4/6-independent and unprotected)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (February 2021) — commercially available as Cosela

treatments.name: Consolidative Thoracic Radiotherapy (post-chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 RCT (CREST trial) supporting consolidative thoracic RT after response to chemotherapy. NCCN guidelines include as option (Category 2A). Evidence in immunotherapy era from emerging studies.

survival_benefit.rationale: CREST: 2-year OS 13% with RT vs 3% without. 6-month PFS 24% vs 7%. Benefit most clear in patients with residual thoracic disease after chemo response. Not curative in extensive stage but can prolong control.

accessibility.rationale: Radiation therapy universally available. Standard 30 Gy in 10 fractions protocol. No special equipment beyond standard linear accelerator. Included in NCCN guidelines.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated at 30 Gy/10 fractions. Esophagitis, fatigue, mild pneumonitis. Low risk of significant toxicity with modern techniques. Timing with immunotherapy requires consideration.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker required. Applicable to all ES-SCLC patients with thoracic disease response. TP53/RB1 status irrelevant for radiation response.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Consolidative thoracic radiation delivers targeted ionizing radiation to residual thoracic tumor following systemic chemotherapy response. Induces DNA damage, immunogenic cell death, and may synergize with concurrent immunotherapy maintenance by enhancing neoantigen presentation.

treatments.name: Topotecan — Second-Line Chemotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: FDA approved for second-line SCLC since 1996. Decades of clinical experience. Used as comparator arm in DeLLphi-304 (73% of chemo arm received topotecan). Well-established efficacy data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS ~6-8 months in second-line. ORR ~24% in sensitive relapse, ~10% in resistant relapse. Now clearly inferior to tarlatamab (OS 8.3 months chemo arm vs 13.6 months tarlatamab in DeLLphi-304). Modest activity in a difficult-to-treat population.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved, generic available, universally accessible worldwide. Oral formulation also available. Low cost. No biomarker testing required.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant myelosuppression: grade 3-4 neutropenia ~70%, thrombocytopenia ~30%, anemia ~40%. Grade 3+ AEs in 80% of patients (DeLLphi-304 comparator). Alopecia, fatigue, diarrhea. Poor tolerability limits treatment duration.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker required. Universal applicability. However, platinum-sensitive vs resistant relapse affects response probability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Topotecan is a topoisomerase I inhibitor that stabilizes the cleavable complex between topoisomerase I and DNA, causing single-strand breaks that lead to cell death during DNA replication. Available as IV and oral formulations.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (1996) — generic available, universally accessible

treatments.name: Ifinatamab Deruxtecan (I-DXd/DS-7300) — B7-H3 ADC

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 IDeate-Lung01 with 137 patients showing 48.2% ORR. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation August 2025. Phase 3 IDeate-Lung02 ongoing with ~540 patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 2: confirmed ORR 48.2%, DCR 87.6%, median PFS 4.9 months, median OS 10.3 months in heavily pretreated population. Among the highest response rates seen in relapsed SCLC.

accessibility.rationale: Not yet FDA approved. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation granted August 2025. Phase 3 IDeate-Lung02 actively enrolling. Access via clinical trial or compassionate use.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ neutropenia (13.9%), lymphopenia (12.4%), anemia (10.2%). Treatment-related ILD/pneumonitis in 12.4% including 2 grade 5 events — a significant safety concern requiring close monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: B7-H3 is highly expressed in SCLC (>85% of tumors). No companion diagnostic required. Expression is independent of TP53/RB1 status. Broad applicability to SCLC population.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Ifinatamab deruxtecan is a B7-H3-directed antibody-drug conjugate consisting of a humanized anti-B7-H3 monoclonal antibody conjugated to a topoisomerase I inhibitor (DXd) payload via a cleavable linker. It delivers cytotoxic payload selectively to B7-H3-expressing tumor cells with bystander killing effect.

key_evidence.journal: IASLC 2025 World Conference on Lung Cancer

biomarker_requirements: B7-H3 expression (present in >85% of SCLC; no companion diagnostic required)
notable_side_effects: interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis (12.4%, including grade 5 events)
treatments.availability: FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation (August 2025) — Phase 3 IDeate-Lung02 enrolling

treatments.name: Sacituzumab Govitecan — TROP2-directed ADC

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 TROPiCS-03 basket trial with 43 SCLC patients showing 41.9% ORR. Published in Journal of Thoracic Oncology January 2025. Already FDA approved for breast cancer — repurposing pathway.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR 41.9% (18 confirmed partial responses out of 43 patients). Activity regardless of platinum sensitivity. Comparable to lurbinectedin ORR but in a single-arm study. No randomized survival comparison yet available for SCLC.

accessibility.rationale: Not FDA approved for SCLC. FDA approved for breast cancer (Trodelvy) — potential off-label use. Clinical trial access for SCLC indication. No phase 3 SCLC trial announced.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3-4 neutropenia common. Diarrhea can be severe. Well-characterized safety profile from breast cancer experience. UGT1A1 polymorphism may affect toxicity (SN-38 payload metabolism).

biomarker_match.rationale: TROP2 is broadly expressed in SCLC. No companion diagnostic required. Activity seen across subtypes. TP53/RB1 status does not affect TROP2 expression.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Sacituzumab govitecan is a TROP2-directed antibody-drug conjugate linking a humanized anti-TROP2 antibody to SN-38 (active metabolite of irinotecan) via a hydrolyzable CL2A linker. High drug-to-antibody ratio (7.6) enables potent cytotoxic payload delivery with bystander effect.
biomarker_requirements: TROP2 expression (broadly expressed in SCLC; no companion diagnostic)
treatments.availability: Phase 2 completed for SCLC — FDA approved for breast cancer (potential off-label). No Phase 3 SCLC trial announced

treatments.name: Alisertib (MLN8237) — Aurora Kinase A Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Randomized Phase 2 data (alisertib+paclitaxel vs placebo+paclitaxel, n=178). FDA Orphan Drug Designation. Phase 2 PUMA-ALI-4201 monotherapy trial ongoing. Biomarker-enrichment strategy shows promise.

survival_benefit.rationale: Alisertib+paclitaxel: PFS 3.32 vs 2.17 months. In cell cycle regulator mutated subgroup (CDK6, RBL1, RBL2, RB1): PFS 3.68 vs 1.80 months, OS 7.20 vs 4.47 months. Modest overall benefit but enhanced in biomarker-selected patients with RB1 mutations.

accessibility.rationale: Not FDA approved. Available through Phase 2 clinical trial PUMA-ALI-4201 for patients who progressed on platinum-based chemo+immunotherapy. FDA Orphan Drug Designation supports accelerated path.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable toxicity profile. Grade 3-4 neutropenia, diarrhea, stomatitis. Myelosuppression is dose-limiting. Paclitaxel combination adds alopecia and neuropathy.

biomarker_match.rationale: This patient's RB1 loss is an enrichment biomarker — patients with cell cycle regulator mutations (including RB1) showed significantly improved outcomes with alisertib. Strong mechanistic rationale for activity in RB1-deficient SCLC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Alisertib selectively inhibits Aurora Kinase A (AURKA), which is essential for mitotic spindle assembly and chromosome segregation. In RB1-deficient SCLC cells, loss of cell cycle checkpoint control creates enhanced dependency on Aurora Kinase A for mitotic fidelity, conferring synthetic lethality.
biomarker_requirements: RB1 loss (enrichment biomarker — enhanced benefit in cell cycle regulator mutated tumors)
treatments.availability: Phase 2 clinical trial PUMA-ALI-4201 (monotherapy in ES-SCLC) — FDA Orphan Drug Designation

treatments.name: Obrixtamig (BI 764532) — DLL3/CD3 Bispecific T-cell Engager

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 dose-escalation completed, published in JCO 2025. Phase 2 DAREON-5 ongoing. FDA Fast Track Designation October 2023. No Phase 3 data yet.

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 1: ORR 28% at optimized dose (Regimens B2/B3), median DOR 8.5 months, 6-month DOR rate 70%. Durable responses in heavily pretreated population. First-line combination trials initiated.

accessibility.rationale: Not FDA approved. Available via clinical trials (NCT05879978 combo with ezabenlimab, NCT06132113 with first-line chemo). FDA Fast Track Designation. Competing with tarlatamab in same DLL3 space.

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable tolerability in Phase 1. CRS is the key toxicity. IgG-like format with longer half-life enables less frequent dosing (Q3W). Step-up dosing mitigates CRS risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets DLL3, highly expressed in >80% of SCLC. DLL3 expression associated with neuroendocrine differentiation. TP53/RB1 loss correlates with DLL3 positivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Obrixtamig is a DLL3/CD3 IgG-like bispecific T-cell engaging antibody with extended half-life compared to BiTE format. Bridges DLL3+ tumor cells with CD3+ T cells to induce tumor cell lysis. IgG-like structure allows Q3W dosing.
biomarker_requirements: DLL3 expression (>80% of SCLC; required for trial enrollment)
treatments.availability: Phase 1/2 clinical trials — FDA Fast Track Designation (October 2023)

treatments.name: EZH2 Inhibitor (DS-3201b/Valemetostat) + Irinotecan

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 study NCT03879798 testing EZH1/2 inhibitor DS-3201b with irinotecan in recurrent SCLC. Preclinical data showing EZH2 inhibition augments chemotherapy and prevents resistance. EZH2 more highly expressed in SCLC than any other tumor type.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical efficacy data published. Preclinical xenograft models show EZH2 inhibition prevents acquired chemotherapy resistance and resensitizes resistant SCLC. Compelling biological rationale but unvalidated clinically.

accessibility.rationale: Clinical trial only (NCT03879798). DS-3201b approved in Japan for lymphoma but not for SCLC. Years from potential SCLC approval.

safety_profile.rationale: EZH2 inhibitors have manageable toxicity from lymphoma experience. Irinotecan adds GI toxicity and myelosuppression. Combination safety in SCLC being characterized.

biomarker_match.rationale: EZH2 expression is highest in SCLC among all cancer types. May be particularly relevant in SLFN11-low tumors that are chemoresistant. TP53/RB1 loss enhances EZH2 dependency in SCLC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Valemetostat (DS-3201b) is a dual EZH1/EZH2 inhibitor that blocks the PRC2 complex responsible for H3K27 methylation. In SCLC, EZH2-mediated epigenetic silencing suppresses SLFN11 (a determinant of chemo-sensitivity). EZH2 inhibition re-expresses SLFN11 and restores chemotherapy sensitivity, particularly to irinotecan.

treatments.name: Bomedemstat (LSD1 inhibitor) + Atezolizumab Maintenance

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 clinical trial ongoing (NCT05191797). Preclinical evidence supports LSD1 inhibition enhancing immunotherapy. No efficacy data published yet for SCLC.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical survival data available. Preclinical models show LSD1 inhibition reactivates immune signaling and enhances checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. Theoretical basis strong but unproven clinically.

accessibility.rationale: Clinical trial only (NCT05191797). Early-phase development. Multiple years from potential approval. Very limited access.

safety_profile.rationale: LSD1 inhibitors have shown manageable toxicity in early trials. Thrombocytopenia is a class effect. Combination with immunotherapy may increase immune-related AEs. Safety profile still being characterized.

biomarker_match.rationale: LSD1 is overexpressed in neuroendocrine tumors including SCLC. TP53/RB1-mutant SCLC may have particular vulnerability to epigenetic reprogramming through LSD1 inhibition.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Bomedemstat inhibits lysine-specific demethylase 1 (LSD1), an epigenetic enzyme that maintains neuroendocrine differentiation and suppresses immune signaling in SCLC. LSD1 inhibition de-represses immune-related genes and Notch pathway, potentially converting immune-cold SCLC to immune-hot phenotype and synergizing with checkpoint blockade.
biomarker_requirements: Newly diagnosed ES-SCLC (maintenance after first-line chemo-immunotherapy)

treatments.name: JBI-802 (Dual HDAC6/LSD1 Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical data showing anti-tumor activity in SCLC models including MYC-amplified variants. Phase 1 clinical trial initiated. Novel dual-target approach.

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical activity only — no clinical efficacy data. Demonstrated ability to inhibit neuroendocrine tumor growth in animal models. Very early stage of development.

accessibility.rationale: Phase 1 clinical trial. Multiple years from any potential approval. Very limited access — select academic centers only.

safety_profile.rationale: Safety profile being characterized in Phase 1. HDAC inhibitors have known class effects (thrombocytopenia, GI toxicity, fatigue). Dual targeting may modify toxicity profile.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets CoREST complex components (HDAC6 + LSD1) central to neuroendocrine differentiation. SCLC with TP53/RB1 loss relies on epigenetic programs maintaining neuroendocrine identity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: JBI-802 is a small-molecule dual inhibitor targeting both HDAC6 and LSD1 components of the CoREST epigenetic complex. Simultaneous inhibition disrupts the epigenetic machinery maintaining neuroendocrine differentiation in SCLC, potentially inducing tumor cell differentiation and growth arrest. Active in both normal and MYC-amplified SCLC variants.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 3 Study of Tarlatamab vs Chemotherapy in Previously Treated ES-SCLC
clinical_trials.status: Completed — led to FDA traditional approval November 2025
clinical_trials.early_results: OS 13.6 vs 8.3 months (HR 0.60, p<0.001). ORR 35% vs 20%. Established tarlatamab as new 2L standard.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 3 Study of Ifinatamab Deruxtecan vs Physician's Choice in Relapsed SCLC
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 2 IDeate-Lung01: ORR 48.2%, DCR 87.6%, median PFS 4.9 months, median OS 10.3 months. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation August 2025.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 3 Study of Lurbinectedin ± Irinotecan as Second-Line Treatment for SCLC
biomarker_requirements: ES-SCLC progressed after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy
clinical_trials.early_results: Confirmatory trial for lurbinectedin. Results pending — may convert accelerated approval to full approval.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Alisertib Monotherapy in ES-SCLC After Platinum-Based Chemo+Immunotherapy
biomarker_requirements: Biomarker-defined subgroups being evaluated (including RB1 loss)
clinical_trials.early_results: Prior Phase 2 randomized data: PFS 3.32 vs 2.17 months with alisertib+paclitaxel vs placebo+paclitaxel. Enhanced benefit in RB1-mutant subgroup (OS 7.20 vs 4.47 months).

clinical_trials.title: Obrixtamig + Ezabenlimab (PD-1 inhibitor) in DLL3+ SCLC and NECs
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 1 monotherapy: ORR 28%, median DOR 8.5 months. Now testing combination with anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor.

clinical_trials.title: Bomedemstat (LSD1 inhibitor) + Atezolizumab Maintenance in Newly Diagnosed ES-SCLC
biomarker_requirements: Newly diagnosed ES-SCLC (maintenance after first-line response)
clinical_trials.early_results: No clinical results yet. Preclinical data shows LSD1 inhibition enhances immunotherapy by reactivating immune signaling in SCLC.

clinical_trials.title: DS-3201b (EZH1/2 inhibitor) + Irinotecan in Recurrent SCLC
biomarker_requirements: Recurrent SCLC after prior systemic therapy
clinical_trials.early_results: No clinical results yet. Preclinical evidence shows EZH2 inhibition resensitizes chemoresistant SCLC to topoisomerase inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Trilaciclib protects hematopoietic stem cells from chemotherapy-induced myelosuppression by inducing transient G1 arrest via CDK4/6 inhibition. RB1-deficient SCLC cells are CDK4/6-independent and remain chemosensitive. Reduces grade 4 neutropenia from ~50% to ~2%, enabling better dose intensity and potentially improved outcomes. Especially relevant for this patient with RB1 loss.

After induction with standard chemo-immunotherapy, switching to lurbinectedin + atezolizumab maintenance extends PFS (5.4 vs 2.1 months) and OS (13.2 vs 10.6 months). Lurbinectedin's tumor-associated macrophage depletion creates an immunopermissive microenvironment that synergizes with continued checkpoint blockade.

Radiation induces immunogenic cell death and enhances neoantigen presentation. Concurrent or sequential RT with immunotherapy maintenance may convert residual thoracic disease into an in situ vaccination effect. Upfront low-dose RT with atezolizumab+chemo showed efficacy in early trials.

Adding DLL3-targeted BiTE therapy to first-line platinum-etoposide+immunotherapy could leverage chemotherapy-induced tumor antigen release to enhance BiTE-mediated T-cell killing. Trials evaluating concurrent bispecific + chemo-IO from cycle 1 are underway.

Aurora Kinase A is essential for mitotic spindle assembly. RB1-deficient SCLC cells depend on Aurora Kinase A for cell cycle progression, creating synthetic lethality. Paclitaxel stabilizes microtubules, complementing Aurora Kinase A inhibition for synergistic mitotic catastrophe. This patient's RB1 loss makes this combination particularly relevant.

EZH2 inhibition reactivates SLFN11 expression which is a key determinant of chemosensitivity in SCLC. Preclinical data shows EZH2 inhibitors can overcome acquired chemoresistance and resensitize SCLC to topoisomerase inhibitors and platinum agents, potentially enabling effective rechallenge.

supportive_care.evidence: 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 2 trials in ES-SCLC. FDA approved February 2021. NCCN guideline-listed.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces grade 4 neutropenia from ~50% to ~2%. Decreases RBC transfusion rates, G-CSF use, and chemotherapy dose reductions. Protects immune cell function during chemotherapy. Particularly beneficial for this patient with RB1 loss (CDK4/6-independent tumor).

supportive_care.approach: Prophylactic Cranial Irradiation (PCI) vs MRI Surveillance
supportive_care.evidence: EORTC Phase 3 trial showed PCI reduces brain metastasis incidence (33% vs 59% at 3 years). However, Japanese trial and immunotherapy-era data show no OS benefit when routine MRI surveillance is used. NCCN Category 2A.
supportive_care.benefit: PCI reduces brain metastasis incidence but at cost of neurocognitive decline. Alternative: serial brain MRI every 3 months with early SRS/WBRT upon detection. For this young (45 y.o.) patient with good performance status, MRI surveillance may preserve neurocognitive function while achieving similar OS outcomes.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Moderate — discuss PCI vs MRI surveillance with patient (controversial in immunotherapy era)

supportive_care.approach: Glycemic Management During Immunotherapy and Corticosteroids
supportive_care.evidence: Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause new-onset type 1 diabetes or worsen existing type 2 diabetes. Corticosteroids used for CRS management (tarlatamab) or immune-related AE treatment significantly impair glycemic control.
supportive_care.benefit: Proactive endocrinology co-management reduces hyperglycemic emergencies, diabetic ketoacidosis risk, and treatment interruptions. Close HbA1c monitoring (every 6-8 weeks), glucose monitoring during steroid pulses, and insulin dose adjustment protocols are essential for this patient with T2DM.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong (standard of care for diabetic patients on immunotherapy)

supportive_care.evidence: Temel et al., NEJM 2010 — landmark RCT of 151 patients with metastatic NSCLC showed early palliative care improved OS (11.6 vs 8.9 months, p=0.02) and QoL. American College of Chest Physicians recommends early palliative care for all ES-SCLC patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Improves quality of life, reduces depression, and paradoxically improves overall survival by 2-3 months. Addresses symptom management, goals-of-care conversations, and psychosocial support. Particularly important for extensive-stage disease with median OS ~12-15 months.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN guidelines recommend primary G-CSF prophylaxis with regimens having >20% febrile neutropenia risk. EP-based chemotherapy for SCLC carries ~15-25% febrile neutropenia risk. Real-world data: G-CSF used in 47% of ES-SCLC patients.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces febrile neutropenia episodes, hospitalizations, chemotherapy dose delays/reductions, and treatment-related mortality. Essential when trilaciclib is not available. Pegfilgrastim or filgrastim per institutional protocol.

supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses show structured exercise programs during lung cancer treatment improve fatigue, physical function, and quality of life. Nutritional support reduces cachexia-related morbidity.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains ECOG performance status (currently 0), reduces treatment-related fatigue, improves cardiovascular fitness, and helps glycemic control for this patient with T2DM. Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation may preserve lung function.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Moderate (supportive evidence from meta-analyses, ASCO endorsed)
